questionText: Tell me about a time when you had to make a quick decision without all the data. (Customer Obsession / Bias for Action)

starFramework.Situation: Describe a context where a deadline was tight and critical metrics or customer experience was at risk.
starFramework.Task: Identify the data that was missing and why waiting for it would have caused negative consequences.
starFramework.Action: Detail your calculation of risks, mitigation plans, and the action you took immediately.
starFramework.Result: State the business outcome, what you learned, and how you corrected course if needed.

expectedPoints: Demonstrated Bias for Action while managing risk
Calculated trade-offs of waiting vs. moving forward
commonMistakes: Making a blind guess without any logical mitigation
Blaming others or external circumstances for the lack of data
Failing to verify the outcome afterwards
strongExample: In my previous project, we saw a sudden 5% drop in checkout conversions after a release. We didn't have telemetry on which service failed, but we knew rollback was the safest action for the customer. I authorized the rollback within 3 minutes, resolving the leak, and then investigated the telemetry logs which confirmed a database connection timeout.
weakExample: Our database went slow. I didn't know why, so I decided to restart the server. It worked but we lost some transaction logs. I didn't think to back up because we were in a rush.

questionText: Describe a situation where you had a conflict with a teammate. How did you resolve it?

starFramework.Situation: Describe a work context where a difference of opinions on a design or priority occurred.
starFramework.Task: Identify the cause of friction and how it risked impacting the project milestones.
starFramework.Action: Detail how you scheduled a 1-on-1, separated opinions from objective metrics, and focused on target results.
starFramework.Result: Explain how you resolved the conflict, reached a consensus, and improved the relationship.

expectedPoints: Focused on objective data rather than emotions
commonMistakes: Portraying yourself as completely right and the teammate as wrong
Involving management immediately before attempting 1-on-1 resolution
strongExample: We were designing a service architecture. A colleague preferred standard REST while I wanted gRPC for payload efficiency. Instead of arguing, we set up a micro-benchmark project. The data showed gRPC saved 40% bandwidth but REST saved 20% dev time. Since our budget was tight but latency wasn't critical, we compromised on REST with JSON compression, maintaining project velocity.
weakExample: A peer changed my code without telling me. I was angry and told them to revert it. They said their way was better. We didn't talk for a week until our lead stepped in.

questionText: Tell me about a time when you had to work under a tight deadline.

starFramework.Situation: Describe a project task or exam with a very close submission date.
starFramework.Task: Detail the requirements and what needed to be accomplished.
starFramework.Action: Explain how you prioritized tasks, created a schedule, and put in extra hours.
starFramework.Result: State that you completed the project on time with good results and positive feedback.

commonMistakes: Admitting you missed the deadline without a valid excuse
Not detailing the actions you took to prioritize
strongExample: During our college capstone project, a core member fell ill three days before submission. I quickly scheduled a call with the rest of the team. We split his modules based on our strengths, re-prioritized the tasks, and worked late. We submitted the project 4 hours before the deadline and scored an A grade.
weakExample: We had a project due tomorrow. I had to stay up all night drinking coffee. I was tired but I got it done.

questionText: Tell me about a time you failed and what you learned from it.

starFramework.Situation: Detail a real work/academic situation that did not achieve its goal.
starFramework.Task: Identify your failure or mistake and why it happened.
starFramework.Action: Explain how you took responsibility, corrected the issue, and set up protections.
starFramework.Result: Describe what you learned, how it made you a better engineer, and the successful outcome next time.

expectedPoints: Took personal ownership of the failure
Actionable learning outcomes integrated into future work
commonMistakes: Sharing a 'fake' failure that is actually a success
strongExample: In my college project, I pushed an API key to a public GitHub repo. The key was scraped, costing us our free tier limit. I immediately revoked the key, rotated our secrets, and setup git-secrets locally. I also wrote a short guide for the team on environment variables, preventing future leaks.
weakExample: We missed our deadline because the client kept changing their mind. I tried my best but they weren't clear.
